sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content: Gastrointestinal stromal tumors (GIST) are the most common soft tissue sarcoma of the gastrointestinal (GI) tract. Patients with a suspected GIST may present with a variety of symptoms, which may include early satiety, abdominal discomfort due to pain or swelling, intraperitoneal hemorrhage, GI bleeding, or fatigue related to anemia. Some patients may present with an acute abdomen, which requires immediate medical attention [MS-2]. GIST can arise anywhere along the GI tract, but stomach (60%) and small intestine (30%) are the most common primary sites. Duodenum (4%–5%) and rectum (4%) are less common, and only a small number of cases have been reported in the esophagus (<1%) and colon and appendix (1%–2%) [6].

symptoms.name: Early satiety
symptoms.detail: May occur due to mass effect, especially with gastric GIST.

symptoms.name: Abdominal discomfort/pain
symptoms.detail: Can be due to tumor size, local growth, or swelling.

symptoms.name: GI bleeding
symptoms.detail: May present as melena or hematemesis; can be overt or occult leading to anemia.

symptoms.name: Intraperitoneal hemorrhage
symptoms.detail: Acute event, often due to tumor rupture.

symptoms.name: Fatigue
symptoms.detail: Often related to chronic blood loss and subsequent anemia.

symptoms.name: Acute abdomen
symptoms.detail: Resulting from tumor rupture, GI obstruction, or peritonitis-like pain.
symptoms.frequency: Less common but requires immediate attention [MS-2]

symptoms.name: Palpable abdominal mass
symptoms.detail: May be discovered on physical examination, especially with larger tumors.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: May present as melena or hematemesis; can be overt or occult leading to anemia.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Acute event, often due to tumor rupture.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Resulting from tumor rupture, GI obstruction, or peritonitis-like pain.
